entry.title: POAC - PO Accruals proposal run and approvals captures fully settled service POs
entry.content:
Hi Experts

I am using SAP S4HANA Private Cloud 2023 with 02/2025 upgrade. I have activated POAC - Purchase order accruals recently. Review and approval are also  activated for ACCRL item. I noticed for certain Service POs (Item Category D), which are fully SES and Invoiced, gets picked by the PO accruals engine.  Planned accrual method is PLN_DELSCH.

Eg - PO 45000123 created date 02.10.2025, SES/ GRN date 27.10.2025 - Amount USD 11,000

When I run the POAC proposal (ACEPROPOSALRUN) for 31.07.2026, this PO gets captured with,

Actual period amount - 0.00

Planned period amount - USD 11,000

If the PO is fully GRNed and Invoiced, why does the POAC accrual program picks this PO and why it happens only for certain POs? Has anyone come across something like this in purchase order accruals?

Thanks

Kera

entry.title: Getting Started with SAP Snowflake in Business Data Cloud: Build Your First ML Model
entry.content:
With the recently announced partnership, Snowflake is now available as a Solution Extension inside SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Together with existing offerings such as SAP HANA Cloud ML capabilities (PAL/APL), SAP Databricks, and the RPT-1.5 model, it represents another major building block in SAP's growing AI, machine learning, and data science ecosystem. This unlocks a powerful combination: your trusted SAP business data on one side, and Snowflake's AI Data Cloud with its rich ML and Cortex AI capabilities on the other - all governed, integrated, and without the traditional ETL headaches.

In this hands-on getting-started guide, we'll walk through your very first end-to-end ML project on Snowflake through BDC. We'll:

  1. Access Snowflake through SAP BDC
  2. Manually upload the classic Iris dataset
  3. Create a Snowflake Notebook and explore its Markdown, SQL, and Python cell types
  4. Use the Cortex AI Copilot to generate a new cell for us
  5. Train a simple Random Forest classifier using snowflake-ml-python
  6. Visualize the results - all without leaving the notebook

Before we start, I'd like to thank @Tobias_Mueller and @AndreasForster for their support with this blog. Now, let's dive in.

Table of contents

  • The Use Case
  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema
  • Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually
  • Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook
  • Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types
  • Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot
  • Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML
  • Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)
  • Step 9 - What Else You Should Know
  • Conclusion & Next Steps

The Use Case

For this getting-started guide, we'll use the classic Iris flower dataset - a small, clean dataset of 150 flower samples, each described by four measurements (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width) and labeled with one of three species: setosa, versicolor, or virginica. Our goal is a simple multi-class classification problem: predict the species of a flower from its measurements.

Why Iris? It's the "Hello World" of machine learning. Small enough to train in seconds, yet rich enough to demonstrate a complete end-to-end ML workflow: data ingestion, exploration, model training, and evaluation. This lets us focus on how the SAP BDC × Snowflake platform works rather than on data wrangling.

But think of Iris as a stand-in for your real SAP business data. The exact same pattern - take structured records, engineer features, train a classifier, and score new data natively in Snowflake - maps directly onto high-value enterprise scenarios, for example:

      • Customer churn prediction on CRM / Sales Cloud data
      • Demand forecasting on S/4HANA sales and inventory data
      • Employee attrition modeling on SuccessFactors data
      • Invoice or expense anomaly detection on Ariba / finance data

In each case, the four "petal and sepal measurements" simply become your business features (e.g., contract length, order frequency, tenure), and the "species" becomes your business outcome (e.g., churn / no-churn). Once you've completed this walkthrough with Iris, you'll have the blueprint to swap in genuine SAP data products and derive real insights - without moving data out of your governed landscape.

Architecture

Before we get hands-on, let's understand where Snowflake fits within the SAP Business Data Cloud landscape.

The Big Picture: Snowflake as a BDC Solution Extension

At the highest level, SAP Business Data Cloud unifies your SAP data sources (S/4HANA, HCM, SuccessFactors, Concur, and more) into a governed Business Data Fabric. SAP Snowflake plugs in as a Solution Extension, bringing Zero-Copy data access and Custom AI/ML capabilities right alongside SAP BW PCE, the BDC Cockpit, and SAP Datasphere - all consuming from a shared object store of SAP and custom data products.

The End-to-End Flow: From SAP Data to ML Insights

In a full production scenario, the journey of data looks like this: SAP data products are defined at the source (e.g., S/4HANA PCE), exposed through the SAP Foundation Service, and shared into the BDC Cockpit / SAP Datasphere Data Catalog. From there, they flow zero-copy into the SAP Snowflake Unity/Horizon Catalog, where a Notebook trains a model and produces an ML Dataset. That dataset can then be shared back to SAP Datasphere and consumed by analytic models, views, or a story in SAP Analytics Cloud - closing the loop from raw SAP data to actionable ML insight.

 

Our Focus for This Guide

That end-to-end flow is powerful, but it involves several SAP components working together. For this getting-started guide, we'll keep things simple and stay entirely within SAP Snowflake. Uploading data manually and training a model in a notebook so we can focus purely on learning the tool itself. Once you're comfortable here, the zero-copy integration with the rest of BDC is the natural next step.

 

Prerequisites

      • Access to SAP Business Data Cloud with the Snowflake Solution Extension enabled
      • A Snowflake account/warehouse provisioned through BDC
      • Basic knowledge of SQL and Python
      • The Iris dataset CSV (download from this GitHub gist)

Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud

From your SAP BDC launchpad, navigate to the Snowflake Solution Extension tile. This opens Snowflake's Snowsight UI, already authenticated with your SAP identity. No separate login, no context switching.

 

💡The Snowflake environment provisioned through BDC is a fully managed Snowflake account, but with governance and identity tied back to your SAP landscape.

Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema

Instead of using SQL commands, we'll create our Snowflake objects through the intuitive Snowsight UI.

Create a Warehouse

      1. In Snowsight, navigate to Home.
      2. Click + Create Warehouse.
      3. Configure your warehouse:
        • Name: ML_DEMO_WH
        • Size: X-Small (recommended for this demo)
        • Auto Suspend: 60 minutes
        • Auto Resume: Enabled (default)
      4. Click Create Warehouse.

Create a Database and Schema

      1. Navigate to Catalog → Database Explorer (or Data → Databases).
      2. Click + Database and enter the name ML_DEMO_DB, then click Create.
      3. Open your new ML_DEMO_DB database, click + Schema, enter the name IRIS, and click Create.

 

Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually

Now let's get our training data into Snowflake. No scripts, just clicks. There are two ways to reach the data loading wizard:

      • Option A: From Home, click + Upload local files.
      • Option B: Navigate to Ingestion → Add Data → Load data into a table.

Either path opens the same load-data wizard. From there:

      1. Select the ML_DEMO_WH warehouse on the top right of the pop-up window
      2. Choose the ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS schema and enter a new table name: IRIS_DATA.
      3. Drag and drop your iris.csv file into the wizard.
      4. Snowflake will auto-detect the schema. Confirm the column types (four NUMBER features and one VARCHAR species column).
      5. Click Load.

 

You can verify the upload by clicking on View table details. Here you'll see a preview of your data, can check out the Data Quality or Table Details and explore the Lineage of the IRIS_DATA table.

 

Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook

Navigate to Projects → Workspaces (My Workspace) → + Add new → Notebook.

      • Name: iris_ml_demo
      • Location: ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS
      • Connected Service: Use a Service with Python 3.12 v2.3, a compute pool with at least CPU_X64_S (ideal for hosting multiple services/jobs while saving cost) & Artifact SNOWPARK
      • Warehouse: ML_DEMO_WH

 

Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types

Snowflake Notebooks support three different cell types. You can add the one you need by clicking the corresponding button at the bottom of the notebook, or by inserting a new cell and changing its type using the dropdown in the top-left corner. Let's explore all three.

Markdown Cell

# Iris Classification with Snowflake ML
This notebook trains a Random Forest classifier on the Iris dataset,
using data stored natively in Snowflake and the `snowflake-ml-python` library.

SQL Cell

Name this cell & its output iris_sql (the name matters. We'll reference it from Python):

SELECT * FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA;

Add a second SQL cell for a quick sanity check:

SELECT species, 
       AVG(sepal_length) AS avg_sepal_length,
       AVG(petal_length) AS avg_petal_length
FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA
GROUP BY species;

Python Cell

Here's the magic. You can reference the SQL cell's result directly:

# Reference the SQL cell's result directly and make a working copy
df = iris_sql.copy()
print(df.shape)
df.head()

 

Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot

Snowflake Notebooks come with a built-in Cortex AI Copilot. Click the ✨ (Ask Copilot) icon in the sidebar to open the chat panel.

Try a prompt like:

"Generate a Python cell that plots the distribution of each numeric feature grouped by species using matplotlib."

Cortex will produce something like:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
species_list = df['SPECIES'].unique()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    for species in species_list:
        subset = df[df['SPECIES'] == species]
        axes[i].hist(subset[col], alpha=0.6, label=species, bins=15)
    axes[i].set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')
    axes[i].set_xlabel(col)
    axes[i].set_ylabel('Frequency')
    axes[i].legend()

plt.suptitle('Feature Distributions by Species', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Click Keep all so the newly generated python cell is inserted in the Notebook. No copy-paste needed.

 

💡Try other prompts like "Split the data into train and test sets" or "Show me a correlation heatmap of the features".

Fix an Error with Cortex

Copilot doesn't just generate code - it can also fix it. To demonstrate this, we'll intentionally introduce a typo in the following Python cell. The misspelled describe() method (descibe) will trigger an AttributeError, allowing us to see how Cortex identifies and fixes the issue.

# Generate a statistical summary of the numeric features
numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
summary = df[numeric_cols].descibe()
print(summary)

Run the cell to trigger the intentional AttributeError. Rather than debugging it manually, click the ✨Fix error button that appears alongside the error. Cortex reads the traceback, pinpoints the problem, and suggests the corrected code which you can apply with a single click.

 

Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML

You may have noticed we never had to install any packages. That's because Snowflake Notebooks come with ~100 of the most popular data-science and ML packages preinstalled, along with 1st-party Snowflake packages such as snowflake-snowpark-python.

Before we dive into training, let's quickly confirm the packages we need are available. This is just a check, not an import:

from importlib.metadata import version, PackageNotFoundError

for pkg in ["snowflake-ml-python", "pandas", "scikit-learn", "matplotlib"]:
    try:
        print(f"✅ {pkg}: {version(pkg)}")
    except PackageNotFoundError:
        print(f"❌ {pkg}: NOT available")

Now the fun part. The snowflake.ml.modeling library brings a scikit-learn–like API directly onto Snowflake, so training runs in Snowflake.

from snowflake.snowpark.context import get_active_session
from snowflake.ml.modeling.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

session = get_active_session()
session.sql("USE DATABASE ML_DEMO_DB").collect()
session.sql("USE SCHEMA IRIS").collect()

# Load data as a Snowpark DataFrame
snow_df = session.table("IRIS_DATA")

# Split into train and test
train_df, test_df = snow_df.random_split(weights=[0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

# Define feature columns and target
FEATURE_COLS = ["SEPAL_LENGTH", "SEPAL_WIDTH", "PETAL_LENGTH", "PETAL_WIDTH"]
LABEL_COL = "SPECIES"
OUTPUT_COL = "PREDICTED_SPECIES"

# Train the model
model = RandomForestClassifier(
    input_cols=FEATURE_COLS,
    label_cols=LABEL_COL,
    output_cols=OUTPUT_COL,
    n_estimators=100,
    random_state=42,
)
model.fit(train_df)

# Predict on the test set
predictions = model.predict(test_df)
predictions.select(FEATURE_COLS + [LABEL_COL, OUTPUT_COL]).show(10)

When you run this cell, Snowflake spins up the training in-database. After fit() completes, predict() appends a PREDICTED_SPECIES column, and the last line prints a 10-row sample so you can eyeball the model's predictions against the actual species.

Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)

Let's evaluate our model with three quick views:

from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pred_pd = predictions.to_pandas()

# 1. Accuracy
acc = accuracy_score(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL])
print(f"✅ Test accuracy: {acc:.3f}")

# 2. Confusion matrix
cm = pd.crosstab(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL],
                 rownames=['Actual'], colnames=['Predicted'])
print("\nConfusion matrix:")
print(cm)

# 3. Feature importances
sk_model = model.to_sklearn()
importances = pd.Series(sk_model.feature_importances_, index=FEATURE_COLS)\
                .sort_values()

importances.plot(kind='barh', title='Feature Importances', figsize=(6, 3))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

You'll typically see an accuracy around 0.93–1.00 on Iris, with petal length and petal width as the top features.

 

That's it! A full ML workflow, entirely inside Snowflake, launched from SAP Business Data Cloud.

Step 9 - What Else You Should Know

Before you go, here are two handy features tucked away in the notebook's Tools Panel at the very bottom of the screen. They are completely independent from each other.

Explore Cell Dependencies in the Tools Panel

Open the Tools Panel at the very bottom of the notebook and switch to the Dependencies tab. Here Snowflake visualizes how the cells throughout your notebook are linked with each other. For example, how our Python cell references the iris_sql result, and how later cells build on that DataFrame and the trained model. This dependency view helps you:

  • Understand the flow of data from one cell to the next at a glance.
  • See which cells depend on which, so you know exactly what needs to re-run when you change something upstream.
  • Trace and debug your notebook logic without manually scanning through every cell.

Schedule Notebooks with the Job Scheduler

The Job Scheduler is another capability in the notebook. With it, you can turn your notebook into a recurring job - set a schedule (e.g., hourly or daily), pick a warehouse, and let Snowflake run it automatically. This is perfect for repeatable training or reporting, with no external orchestrator needed.

 

Conclusion & Next Steps

In under 30 minutes, you've gone from "I have SAP BDC access" to "I have a trained ML model running natively in Snowflake." That's the power of the SAP × Snowflake partnership: less plumbing, more insights. While this tutorial focused on building and running ML directly in the SAP Snowflake environment, a production solution would typically continue by writing predictions or enriched datasets back to SAP Business Data Cloud, where they can be consumed across SAP applications.

Where to go next:

      • SAP Business Data Cloud, Snowflake edition
      • Zero-Copy integration between SAP and Snowflake
      • Snowflake ML documentation
      • Snowflake Cortex AI documentation

What's coming next in this series

This is just the beginning. I'm planning a series of follow-up blogs exploring more of what SAP Snowflake has to offer. Here's what's coming soon:

      • Building Streamlit Apps in Snowflake - using the built-in Streamlit functionality to create interactive data apps directly inside Snowflake, no separate hosting required.
      • From SAP-Managed to Custom-Managed Data Products - consuming SAP-managed Data Products, building your own custom-managed Data Products in SAP Snowflake, and sharing them back into the SAP landscape.
      • Bringing SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud ML into SAP Snowflake - leveraging the ML capabilities of SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud from within SAP Snowflake by allowing External Access Integration.

Connect with me on LinkedIn to stay up to date and get notified when the next blogs in this series go live.

Now it's your turn. What SAP data would you love to run an ML model on through Snowflake? Drop your ideas in the comments below.

Happy modeling! 🦾

We identified an issue with an existing Numeric Point widget.

  • Absolute Base Value enabled

  4. Save and refresh the story.
  5. Variance displays without % sign.

 

Additional Observation

If a completely new Numeric Point widget is created using the same measure and comparison settings, the percentage sign is displayed correctly.

Therefore, the issue appears to be related to the rendering or metadata persistence of existing widgets rather than the underlying calculation.

entry.title: 20 Years of Unbroken SAP Certification - How NTT DATA Turns Operations into a Strategic Edge
entry.content:
For more than 35 years, NTT DATA has been shaping how organizations run and transform their SAP landscapes – and their track record speaks for itself. The company has just received the Golden Certificate from SAP, marking their 10th consecutive certification and underscoring an unbroken commitment to quality that few partners can match. In an interview with Lars Janitz – Senior Executive and Head of Global Business & Managed Services at NTT DATA Business Solutions AG, and Head of SAP & ServiceNow for LE – he reveals how NTT DATA helps customers balance rock-solid operations with continuous innovation, without compromising business continuity. From SAP BTP and AI to global security operations, he explains where the greatest opportunities lie in the years ahead. Read on to find out why Lars Janitz believes that a strong operational foundation today is the key to unlocking the full potential of cloud and AI tomorrow.

Could you start by telling us about NTT DATA and the services you provide to customers worldwide?

NTT DATA is a global leader in SAP consulting, implementation, managed services, and business transformation. For more than 35 years, we have been supporting organizations in implementing and modernizing their SAP landscapes, increasing the value of the SAP landscape, moving to modern cloud platforms, and driving business innovation.

What sets us apart is our ability to combine global scale, deep SAP expertise, and local customer proximity. We support customers throughout the entire SAP lifecycle – from strategy and implementation to operations, optimization, and continuous innovation.

Handover of the golden certificate, July 2026

Looking back at your long partnership with SAP, what motivated NTT DATA to pursue its first SAP operations certification in Germany in 2001, and what were the key factors behind expanding that approach globally in 2006?

Our original motivation was simple: to provide customers with independent proof of the quality and reliability of our SAP operations services. As our customers expanded internationally, they expected the same level of service and governance everywhere they operated. Extending the certification approach globally allowed us to create a consistent delivery model, support multinational customers more effectively, and establish the foundation for the global managed services organization we have today.

Many customers are seeking both stability and innovation in their SAP landscapes. How does NTT DATA help customers balance reliable operations with ongoing transformation and modernization?

We don't see stability and innovation as conflicting objectives. Reliable operations and deep industry knowledge create the foundation for successful transformation. Our teams ensure that customers' SAP environments run securely and efficiently while continuously identifying opportunities to improve and automate processes, adopt cloud technologies, and leverage new SAP functionalities. This allows customers to modernize at their own pace without compromising business continuity.

 As SAP continues to accelerate cloud adoption, how is NTT DATA supporting customers on their transformation journey today, and where do you see technologies such as SAP BTP and AI creating the greatest opportunities over the next several years?

We help customers accelerate their cloud journey through a combination of advisory, implementation, managed services, and ongoing optimization. Our goal is to deliver business value, not just technical migration. Furthermore, we leverage our 360-degree partnership with SAP to enhance and innovate our services. 

Looking ahead, SAP BTP and AI will play a critical role in helping organizations integrate processes, extend applications, automate decisions, and unlock greater productivity. The biggest opportunity lies in turning trusted business data into actionable intelligence.

NTT DATA recently achieved global certification for SAP Business AI and SAP security operations. How do these capabilities complement your existing managed services portfolio, and what benefits are customers already seeing today?

These certifications are a natural next step in the evolution of our managed services portfolio. They strengthen our ability to operate SAP environments at scale, intelligently, and securely. For customers, this means faster adoption of AI technologies, stronger security governance, and greater confidence that innovation can be introduced without increasing operational risk.

How do you experience the audits in practice, and what value do they bring to your teams and customers?

We view audits as an opportunity to improve rather than simply a compliance requirement. They provide valuable external validation and help us continuously strengthen our processes, capabilities, and delivery standards. For customers, they provide assurance that our services are regularly assessed against rigorous SAP requirements and industry best practices.

Can you share an example of an insight or recommendation from a past audit that led to a meaningful improvement in the way you deliver services or engage with customers?

One recurring theme has been the importance of globally aligned service management combined with strong local execution. Those insights encouraged us to further standardize incident management, reporting, and governance across our delivery organization. As a result, customers benefit from greater transparency, more consistent service experiences, and improved responsiveness across regions.

With a broad portfolio of SAP operations certifications across multiple solutions and regions, what does this global consistency mean for customers in their day-to-day operations and transformation projects?

For customers, global consistency means trust and predictability. They know what level of service, governance, and expertise they can expect regardless of location. Whether they are running business-critical operations or executing a major transformation program, our standardized delivery approach helps reduce complexity, mitigate risk, and accelerate outcomes.

Looking ahead, how do you expect demand for SAP Business AI operations to evolve, and how is NTT DATA preparing its managed services organization to support that evolution globally?

We expect demand to increase rapidly as AI moves from experimentation to enterprise-wide adoption. Customers will increasingly need partners that can not only deploy AI but also operate and govern it effectively. We are investing in AI operations capabilities, delivery models, security, and talent development to help customers confidently and responsibly scale SAP Business AI across their organizations.

What advice would you give to organizations that are currently defining their SAP operations strategy for the years ahead?

Our advice is to view operations as a strategic enabler of innovation. A modern operations strategy should balance resilience, security, and efficiency with the ability to rapidly adopt new technologies and provide valuable outcomes. Organizations that build a strong operational foundation today will be much better positioned to take advantage of cloud, AI, and future SAP innovations tomorrow.

 

Information on the SAP certification program for operations partners, as well as an overview of all certified partners, can be found in the SAP Operations Partner Guide.

Visit OPC at SAP.com or on the SAP Partner Portal.

entry.title: SAP Systemstatus / Userstatus
entry.content:
You know when you think you know a lot and then everything feels so unreal, you dont understand nothing anymore,
and you want to get out of the Rabit-Hole(...)

In SAP there are the tables

TJ30/TJ30T (User-Status)
TJ02/Texttable TJ02T (Systemstatus)

According to a Kollege 1 User-Status is uniquely assigned to exactly 1
System-Status. Where/how do I Find this unique link. I mean there must be a table where TJ02-ISTAT
where TJ02-ISTAT is linked to TJ30-ESTAT?

I want to set new User Status of an Object (in this case it is a Network in PS with BAPI)
In object oriented Programming I define Interfaces with constants like this:

BEGIN OF mc_user_status,
released TYPE j_status VALUE 'E0004', "Anwenderstatus Freigegeben
END OF mc_user_status .

Is this a bad approach? Which "Status" or Primary Key "defines" the "State" "RELEASED" ob this Object?
Would this be better

BEGIN OF mc_system_status,
released TYPE j_status VALUE 'I0004', "Systemstatus Freigegeben"
END OF mc_user_status .

Now I determine the user-status which is assigned to the system-Status 'I0004' and this one I set with the BAPI as a new User-Status

entry.title: Re: How to configure the app Monitor Material Coverage to...
entry.content:
Dear @ZZ3987,

The screenshot shows Manage Material Coverage (F0251A). In this version the full graphical Stock/Requirements chart view cannot be enabled through CBC or an SSCUI. F0251A provides only the Stock Availability visualization shown in the Material Information section, not the same detailed chart available in the predecessor app F0251

To enable access to the line chart:

1. Open Monitor Material Coverage – Net Segments (F0247A)

2. Open the user menu and select App Settings

3. Set Manage Material Coverage (F0251A) to OFF

4. Choose OK

5. Return to the material list and open the material coverage details again

6. The system will navigate to Manage Material Coverage (F0251) instead of F0251A

7. In the Stock/Requirements section, select the chart icon shown in the first screenshot to display the line chart

The switch therefore does not enable an additional chart within F0251A. It changes the navigation target:

  • ON – navigation to Manage Material Coverage (F0251A)
  • OFF – navigation to Manage Material Coverage (F0251) where the detailed Stock/Requirements chart is available

This can be considered a standard SAP-provided workaround or alternative navigation path. It does not require custom development but it uses the predecessor application because the required detailed line chart is not available in the same form in F0251A. The setting is user specific so each user who requires this chart must maintain it individually in App Settings

If the business requirement is to have the same detailed Stock/Requirements line chart available directly in F0251A, an improvement request can be submitted through SAP Customer Influence. Before creating a new request, SAP recommends checking whether a similar request already exists and voting for it where applicable. SAP KBA 2963059 describes the process for submitting feature requests for Public Cloud

More details below:

SAP Help – Monitor Material Coverage – Net Segments

SAP Help – Monitor Material Coverage – Net and Individual Segments

SAP Help – Manage Material Coverage (F0251)

SAP Help – Feature Comparison for Material Coverage Apps

SAP KBA 2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Q2 2026: Quarterly Release Highlights for SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management
entry.content:
@RegineSchimmer 

Thanks for the roundup!

One question on the identity side: are there any plans for SAP Cloud Identity
Services / IAS to support MCP's Enterprise-Managed Authorization (the ID-JAG
identity assertion authorization grant, RFC 8693)? It went stable in the MCP
spec in June, Okta already ships it (as Cross App Access), and VS Code supports
it client-side via the mcp.enterpriseManagedAuth.idp setting.

The value: the IdP centrally decides which MCP servers an employee may reach,
and after a one-time SSO sign-in, further MCP servers connect silently —
instead of every MCP server running its own OAuth/browser flow.

This feels timely now that SAP is shipping MCP servers itself (MCP Gateway in
Integration Suite, @cap-js/mcp), but as far as I can tell there's no enterprise
SSO story for them yet. Is ID-JAG on the Cloud Identity Services radar?

entry.title: Re: SAP CPQ Document Storage, Retention, and Archiving -...
entry.content:
Document Storage & Lifecycle in SAP CPQ

1. Storage Mechanism
Generated documents (such as PDF, DOCX, and Excel files) are stored on the file system, not in the database.
SAP CPQ tracks storage usage across the following categories:
• Documents (templates and quotation files)
• Proposals (generated output documents)
• Images
Storage capacity is not configured directly within the SAP CPQ application. It is typically defined contractually and managed at the infrastructure/hosting level.
If there are concerns about storage capacity for a particular tenant, the expected document volume should be reviewed with SAP so that the appropriate storage allocation can be confirmed.

2. Can you generate unlimited PDFs and store them in CPQ without external storage?
From the application perspective, there is no strict application-level limit on the number of generated documents. 
However, using the term “unlimited” is difficult without understanding the expected volume.
If a very large number of generated files are expected, it would be useful to clarify the approximate expected document volume or estimated storage consumption. 
Based on that information, we can consult the internal Ops/infrastructure team to confirm whether the current storage allocation is sufficient or whether additional capacity should be planned.
In general, CPQ should be able to support a large number of generated documents, but for high-volume usage scenarios, it is recommended to validate the expected storage requirements with the infrastructure.

3. How long does a document reside on the CPQ system?
Generated documents remain available in SAP CPQ as long as the associated quote exists, unless they are manually deleted.
When a quote is deleted, the generated documents associated with that quote are also removed.
There is no automatic time-based expiration for generated documents.
Additionally, the application parameter "Disable deletion of generated documents on quote" can be used to prevent users from manually deleting generated documents from a quote.

4. Archiving — can you define a retention period?

SAP CPQ does not provide built-in document archiving functionality.
Specifically:
• There is no configurable document retention period.
• There is no automatic cleanup or archival job.
• There is no mechanism to automatically move documents to an archive after a specified period.
Documents remain available until either:
• the associated quote is deleted, or
• a user manually deletes them (if permitted).


5. Out-of-the-box replication to SharePoint / Confluence / other systems?
SAP CPQ does not provide standard out-of-the-box functionality to archive or replicate generated documents to external document management systems such as:
• Microsoft SharePoint
• Confluence
• Other third-party document repositories
Such integrations would need to be implemented as custom integrations.

entry.title: Re: How to do background scheduling with clean core, including Fiori apps, replacing se38 reports
entry.content:
Thank you for the effort that went into this article and for providing a clear introduction to Application Jobs and how you built an integration with RAP and Fiori. For customers starting net-new developments in ABAP Cloud and RAP, this is certainly valuable guidance.

However, I would like to add some context from the perspective of large installed-base SAP landscapes.

The article starts with the term ERP, but much of the discussion is naturally driven by the viewpoint of new developments designed for ABAP Cloud. As Engineering Lead and Delivery Manager responsible for Job Technologies in ABAP systems, I think it is important to broaden the scope somewhat.

In ABAP-based Private Cloud and On-Premise products, the classic Background Processing framework based on ABAP reports remains fully supported, stable, and compliant with Clean Core principles as a Tier 2 extension approach. It is neither deprecated nor technically incompatible with modern SAP Fiori user experiences. Classical background jobs continue to coexist very successfully with newer UI technologies.

It is also important to recognize that Private Cloud and On-Premise environments currently offer three distinct job-processing frameworks, each designed for different architectural and business requirements. These frameworks are not interchangeable replacements for one another and intentionally provide different feature sets.

While there are certainly scenarios where an Application Job and a classic Background Job can deliver comparable business outcomes, the appropriate choice is ultimately determined by the complete end-to-end business process, the collected requirements, and the target architecture. In many established customer systems there is neither a technical limitation nor a business necessity that requires migration from classic Background Jobs to the Application Job Framework.

Therefore, whenever such a transition is considered, architects and application owners should consciously evaluate the feature scope of the available frameworks and select the one that best matches their requirements. Application Jobs should not be viewed as a universal successor to or replacement for classic Background Jobs in Private Cloud and On-Premise environments.

By design, Application Jobs provide a lightweight scheduling model. This simplicity is beneficial in many scenarios, but it also means that they do not expose the full range of capabilities that have evolved within classical Background Processing over several decades. For example, functionality and operational patterns that depend on mechanisms such as TVARVC require alternative implementations inside the application logic or ABAP classes when using Application Jobs.

In customer and partner discussions, my first question is often:

"What is the actual business or technical driver behind converting an existing classic Background Job into an Application Job?"

In my experience, the most common reasons include:

  1. Development policies favoring object-oriented and class-based programming models, resulting in a preference for Application Jobs.
  2. The scheduling requirements are relatively simple and do not require the advanced capabilities offered by the classical Background Processing framework.
  3. The current Private Cloud or On-Premise system is considered a temporary step, while the long-term strategy is a future transition to SAP Public Cloud, making cloud-ready development models a strategic investment.

Ultimately, neither framework is universally "better." They serve different purposes. The key architectural decision should always be driven by functional requirements, operational needs, lifecycle considerations, and the target deployment environment rather than by the assumption that Application Jobs are the mandatory successor to classical Background Processing.

entry.title: One Configuration Switch Unlocks Invoice Receipt Date Tracking for Every Country
entry.content:
For years, the Invoice Receipt Date on the accounting document header was a assigned to a handful of country localizations like the Czech VAT Ledger. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, that changes: one configuration switch now activates the field for every company code in every country/region.

Scope

Scope Note: This blog post covers SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 and later. The feature is delivered through Configuration Activity 107263 — Activate Invoice Receipt Date, application component FI-LOC-FI, and applies to scope item J60 (Accounts Payable). The activation is a customer-managed setting: no SAP Note or manual coding is required.

Why the Invoice Receipt Date Matters

The Invoice Receipt Date — stored in the classic field BKPF-REINDAT — captures the day your organization actually received a supplier invoice. It is deliberately distinct from the three dates you already know:

  • Document Date (BLDAT) — the date printed on the supplier's invoice.
  • Posting Date (BUDAT) — the date that drives the accounting period.
  • Entry Date (CPUDT) — the system timestamp when the document was entered.

Legislation and internal governance increasingly measure payment performance from the moment the invoice arrives at your door — not from the invoice date and not from the posting date. Examples include the EU Late Payment Directive, the UK Payment Practices Reporting obligation for large companies, and dozens of country-specific VAT and audit rules. Without a dedicated field, teams end up storing the receipt date in a custom Z-field, misusing the baseline date, or reconstructing it from workflow logs — none of which reports cleanly.

Activating the standard field solves all three problems at once: it becomes visible in every relevant Fiori app, it flows into journal entries automatically, and it is available as a first-class field for CDS views, custom reports, and statutory extractors.

Activation in Three Screens

The activation is a linear flow: open the configuration activity, pick the company codes that need it, and post an invoice to confirm the field is live.

Step 1 — Open Configuration Activity 107263

In your configuration environment, search for Activate Invoice Receipt Date (Configuration Activity 107263). The SSCUI is the single entry point for this setting.

Step 2 — Select the Company Codes

Add each company code that should expose the Invoice Receipt Date field. The activation is per company code, so you can pilot the feature in one entity before rolling it out group-wide.

Step 3 — Post a Supplier Invoice to Verify

Open Create Supplier Invoice (F0859) — or any of the FI/AP posting apps — and the new Invoice Receipt Date field appears alongside the Document Date and Posting Date. Once filled, the value is persisted in BKPF-REINDAT and is immediately available for reporting.

Practical Takeaway

To roll this out cleanly:

  • Identify the company codes that need the Invoice Receipt Date (start with entities subject to statutory payment-practice reporting).
  • Open Configuration Activity 107263 in the configuration environment.
  • Activate the setting for the selected company codes.
  • Test with one supplier invoice in F0859 — confirm the field appears and posts to BKPF-REINDAT.
  • Align your AP workflow so the mailroom / OCR / IDP tool populates the field automatically for every incoming invoice.

Summary

The Invoice Receipt Date has finally graduated from a niche public-sector field to a standard journal-entry attribute available to every SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customer. One SSCUI, one flag per company code, and your organization has a compliance-ready foundation for payment-practice reporting and receipt-based payment KPIs — without a custom field in sight.

Key Takeaways

  • Global availability: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 makes the Invoice Receipt Date (BKPF-REINDAT) available in all countries/regions, not just Public Sector or a few localizations.
  • Customer-controlled activation: Configuration Activity 107263 — Activate Invoice Receipt Date turns the field on per company code — no SAP Note, no code change.
  • Standard field, standard reporting: Because the value lives in BKPF-REINDAT, it flows into CDS views, extractors, and statutory reports as a first-class attribute — retiring the custom Z-fields many customers have relied on for years.

More Information

  • SAP Help Portal — Activate Invoice Receipt Date for All Countries/Regions (What's New 2608)
  • SAP Help Portal — Activating the Invoice Receipt Date (Country/Region-Specific Functions)
  • Scope item J60 — Accounts Payable
  • Application component FI-LOC-FI

Published by SAP Globalization Services · Feedback and questions welcome in the comments below.

entry.title: Klonen - aus eins mach viele: Einheit 2 Lektion 3
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Klonen"

In dieser Lektion beschäftigst du dich mit Klonen, um aus einem Objekt ganze Schwärme von Agenten zu erzeugen. Du erfährst außerdem mehr über den Geltungsbereich von Variablen, den sogenannten Scope, und wie du damit effizient Variablen über verschiedene Klone hinweg verwenden kannst. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Mache dein Projekt interaktiv. Füge eine Variable für den Winkel hinzu und verwende diese als Eingabe für den drehe Block.
    Erhöhe den Wert, der in der Variablen gespeichert ist, wenn die rechte Pfeiltaste gedrückt wird und verringere ihn mit der linken Pfeiltaste.  


    Hinweis: Um zu verhindern, dass du die Winkel-Variable von jedem Klon aus anpasst, achte darauf, sie aus dem fortlaufend-Skript zu ändern. Dieses Skript wird nur vom Vorfahr-Objekt ausgeführt.  

    Hinweis: Abhängig davon, wie schnell dein Computer ist, kann die Variable sich mehrmals mit einem Tastendruck ändern. Um das zu verhindern, kannst du warten, bis die Taste nicht mehr gedrückt ist, bevor du die weiteren Blöcke in diesem Skript ausführst.

 

  • Programmiere eine “virtuelle Limonade” Animation. Erstelle ein Blasen-Kostüm und klone dein Objekt fortlaufend.

    Jeder Klon sollte:

    • an einer zufälligen Stelle am unteren Rand der Bühne erscheinen.
    • Seine y-Position ändern, bis er eine bestimmte Position am oberen Rand der Bühne erreicht.
    • sich löschen, wenn er diesen y-Wert überschreitet.  

     Entwickle deine Animation weiter.
    Hier sind einige Ideen, die deine Limonaden-Animation realistischer und spannender machen:

    Setze die Größe der Blasen zufällig.

    Gib jeder Blase einen Farbe-Effekt.

    		 

    Füge eine kleine horizontale Bewegung (x-Achse) hinzu.

    Lasse die Blasen mit verschiedener Geschwindigkeit aufsteigen.
    Entweder zufällig oder basierend auf ihrer Größe – größere Blasen steigen schneller als kleinere.

    Ergänze eine zufällige Wartezeit, bevor die Klone gelöscht werden.

    		 

    Ändere den Hintergrund, damit er dem Farbschema deiner Blasen entspricht.

 

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/z6gYL2A?rc=10&doi=SAP1326859 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/MzxBTT8?rc=10&doi=SAP1326861 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 3 findest du in dieser Collection .

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Erzeugt einen temporären Klon eines Objekts. Dieser Klon wird nicht im Objektbereich angezeigt. Temporäre Klone können per Block oder durch Klicken auf den roten Stopp-Knopf gelöscht werden. 
Wird einmalig ausgeführt, jedesmal wenn ein Klon erzeugt wird.
Das Skript das an diesen Startblock angefügt ist, wird nur vom Klon selbst, nicht aber dem Vorfahr-Objekt ausgeführt. 

Löscht den Klon, der diesen Block ausführt, programmatisch.

Initialisiert eine Skriptvariable, die nur innerhalb des Skriptes gültig ist, in dem sie definiert wurde. 

Skriptvariablen können durch Klick auf die orangene Variable umbenannt werden. Mit den Pfeilköpfen können weitere Skriptvariablen hinzugefügt oder entfernt werden. 

 

Klone

Das Hauptthema dieser Lektion waren Klone. Klone sind sogenannte flache Kopien des ursprünglichen Objekts.

Das bedeutet, dass sie komplexe Eigenschaften wie Skripte, Kostüme, Klänge und Variablen gemeinsam mit ihrem Vorfahr nutzen. Im biologischen Sinne teilen Klone dasselbe „genetische Material“ mit dem Organismus, von dem sie geklont wurden (in diesem Fall sind das die Skripte). Dieses „genetische Material“ kann sich jedoch aufgrund äußerer Bedingungen unterschiedlich ausprägen (in der Biologie z. B. Klima oder Ernährung, in der Informatik z. B. zufällige Eingabewerte).

Programmatisch erzeugte Klone übernehmen die Werte ihres Vorfahr-Objekts für Eigenschaften wie Größe, Kostüm, Position oder Grafikeffekte.

Du kannst den Block Wenn ich als Klon starte verwenden, um Klone zu erzeugen und zu individualisieren, sodass sie ein anderes Kostüm tragen, sich an eine andere Position bewegen oder eine andere Größe haben. Dieses Konzept wird in der Informatik als „Initialisierung“ bezeichnet.

Mit Klonen kannst du Projekte mit vielen Agenten erstellen, die sich ähnlich verhalten. Dadurch lassen sich komplexe Projekte wesentlich schneller programmieren, da du nur ein einziges Objekt modellieren musst, das du anschließend beliebig oft klonen kannst.

 

Skriptvariablen

Du hast bereits in früheren Lektionen objekt-lokale (gelten innerhalb eines Objekts) und globale Variablen (gelten im gesamten Projekt) kennengelernt. In dieser Lektion hast du eine dritte Art von Variablen kennengelernt: Skriptvariablen.

Skriptvariablen haben einen eingeschränkteren Geltungsbereich; sie sind nur innerhalb des Skripts gültig, in dem sie definiert wurden. Außerhalb dieses Skripts kann weder auf sie zugegriffen noch ihr Wert gelesen oder verändert werden.

Skriptvariablen sind besonders nützlich, wenn du sie nur in einem einzelnen Skript verwenden möchtest und ihre Werte außerhalb dieses Skripts nicht benötigt werden. In dieser Lektion haben wir sie verwendet, um die Geschwindigkeit jedes Klons in der Blume individuell festzulegen.

Einer der großen Vorteile von Skriptvariablen ist, dass sie die Blockpalette nicht überladen, da sie keinen dauerhaften Variablen-Reporter in der "Variablen"-Kategorie erhalten.

Du kannst eine Skriptvariable mit dem Block Skriptvariable _ erstellen. Wenn du auf die orangefarbene Variable klickst, öffnet sich ein Dialogfenster, in dem du die Variable umbenennen kannst. Ein Klick auf die Pfeilspitze nach rechts fügt weitere Skriptvariablen hinzu. Der Pfeil nach links löscht die letzte Skriptvariable, wenn mehr als eine vorhanden ist. 

Skriptvariablen können durch Klick auf die orange Variable umbenannt werden. Gib den gewünschten Namen im Dialogfenster ein und klicke auf "OK" um den Namen der Variablen zu ändern.

 

Manchmal ist es hilfreich, die Werte einer Variable zu beobachten, während das Programm ausgeführt wird. Da Skriptvariablen keinen Berichtsblock in der Blockpalette erhalten, kannst du das Kontrollkästchen zum Anzeigen auf der Bühne nicht aktivieren. Es gibt jedoch zwei andere Möglichkeiten, ihre Werte auf der Bühne anzuzeigen.

Du kannst entweder mit der rechten Maustaste auf das Skript klicken, das die Skriptvariable enthält, und den Variablennamen am Ende des Kontextmenüs auswählen. Beachte dabei, dass Skriptvariablen nur existieren, während das Skript ausgeführt wird, in dem sie definiert sind.

Alternativ kannst du die Blöcke zeige Variable und verstecke Variable verwenden. Wähle die Variable aus, die angezeigt werden soll. Sobald der Block ausgeführt wird, erscheint die entsprechende Variablenanzeige auf der Bühne. Um eine bestimmte Variable auszublenden, kannst du sie im Auswahlmenü des Blocks verstecke Variable auswählen. Wenn du das Eingabefeld leer lässt, werden alle temporären Variablen ausgeblendet, die derzeit auf der Bühne angezeigt werden.

Skriptvariablen und ihre Werte können auf der Bühne angezeigt werden mithilfe der zeige Variable und verstecke Variable Blöcke. Alternativ kann die Variable auch vom Kontextmenü des Skripts, in dem sie definiert ist, angezeigt werden. Klicke dafür mit der rechten Maustaste auf das Skript, während es aktiv ist, und wähle den Variablennamen am Ende des Menüs aus.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 2: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/rekursion-einen-baum-wachsen-lassen-einheit-2-lektion-4/ba-p/14442831 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai 

 

Dieses Material ist veröffentlicht unter der CC 4.0 Lizenz durch das Snap! Team bei SAP

entry.title: Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications
entry.content:
Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications

Introduction

As we all know, that we can create Fiori charts easily using the standard templates provided by SAP, but it does not give enough flexibility to customize on top of that, when we want to make any changes in those standard templates then we must make use of custom charts. For custom charts, VizFrames are used to perform the customization on the chart as per the requirement.

In one of my recent implementations, I defined the custom chart using VizFrame with two lines and two columns on x and y axis. Apart from this there was a dynamic colour change option given to the user, so that they can dynamically change colors of both the lines and columns, and the colour once picked, has persisted in the localStorage of the browser for next time use.

This blog explains the implementation steps:

Business Requirement

  • Dual combination chart (2lines and 2 bars)
  • Allow user to personalize chart colours.
  • Saves the colour preference.

Solution

To achieve the above requirement, we have used

  • sap.viz.ui5.controls.VizFrame
  • dual_combinatoin chart type
  • ColorPickerPopover
  • Sap.m.Menu
  • Browser localStorage

Step1: Create a dual combination chart using VizFrame:

The custom chart is created using VizFrame with dual_combination as the chart type.

<viz:VizFrame id="idVizFrame" vizType="dual_combination" uiConfig="{applicationSet:'fiori'}">
</viz:VizFrame>

Step2: Define the chart dimensions and measures:

Here, I am defining the chart dimensions and measures.

Dimension:<vizDataset:DimensionDefinition
                             name="Period"
                             value="{Date}" />

Measure:  <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric A"
                              value="{Value1}" />

                     <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric B"
                             value="{Value2}" />

                   <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric C"
                             value="{Value3}" />

                 <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric D"
                             value="{Value4}" />

Step3: Configuring chart feeds:

First, I have configured the primary axis to display column measures and then the secondary axis to display line measures.

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis" type="Measure" values="Metric A,Metric B"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis2" type="Measure" values="Metric C,Metric D"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="categoryAxis" type="Dimension" values="Period"/>

Step4: Creating colour palette button:

Enabling palette button as drop-down menu, so that the user can select the measure for which personalized colour needs to be picked.

<Button
       icon="sap-icon://palette"
       type="Transparent"
       tooltip="Change Chart Colors"
       press=".onOpenColorSettings"/>

When the user clicks on the menu item, a color selection menu appears.

onOpenColorSettings: function(oEvent) {
        if (!this._oColorMenu) {
            this._oColorMenu = new sap.m.Menu({
                items: [
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({ 
                   text: "Metric A",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric A")   }),
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({
                   text: "Metric B",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric B")  })
               ]
         });
      }
     this.oColorMenu.openBy(oEvent.getSource());
    }

As shown in the snap below, we get a colour palette button, with the dropdown menu and all the required options, and when we select on each one, we get the colour picker as shown in the snap below.

     

Step5: Define the default colours during Initialization:

I have set the default colour values for all measures inside onInit() method.

this.aColors = {
     "Metric A": "#2E86AB",
     "Metric B": "#28B463",
     "Metric C": "#F39C12",
     "Metric D": "#8E44AD"
 };

Step6: Applying personalized colours using dataPointStyle in VizFrame:

I have used VizFrame's dataPointStyle config to apply personalized colours to the measures.

plotArea: {
     dataPointStyle: {
          rules: [
            { 
              dataContext: { measureNames: "Metric A"},
               properties: {color: "#2E86AB"}
            },
           {
               dataContext: {measureNames: "Metric B"},
               properties: {color: "#28B463"}
           }
         ]

Step7: Open color picker when the measure is selected:

When the measure is selected from the menu, a color picker dialog appears.

_openColorPicker: function(oControl, sMeasure) {

        if (!this._oColorPicker) {
             this._oColorPicker = new sap.ui.unified.ColorPickerPopover({
                  colorString: "HEX",
                  change: fuction(oEvent) {
                       var sColor = oEvent.getParameter("colorString");
                        this.applyMeasureColor( sMeasure, sColor );
                  }.bind(this)
             });
           }
       this._oColorPicker.openBy(oControl);
      }

Step8: To dynamically update the VizFrame properties:

The selected color is updated dynamically by updating the VizFrame properties.

_applyMeasureColor: function(sMeasure, sColor) {
          var oVizFrame = this.byId("idVizFrame");
          var oProps = oVizFrame.getVizProperties();
          oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules =
                oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules.map(function(oRule) {
                      if (oRule.dataContext.measureNames === sMeasure) {
                               oRule.properties.color = sColor;
                        }
                   return oRule;
                });
               oVizFrame.setVizProperties(oProps);
    }

Step9: Persist the selected colours using browser local storage:

The user selected colors are stored in the browser's local storage, so that the preferred colors are picked up when you reopen the application.

localStorage.setItem(
        "CHART_COLORS",
          JSON.stringify(oColorObject)
    );

We can retrieve the same during initialization, so that the colours remain consistent even while reopening the application.
 

 JSON.parse(
        localStorage.getItem("CHART_COLORS")
   );

Output

Here is the final working chart snap:

Conclusion

This shows that instead of limiting to pre-defined charts or templates, one can personalize the charts and visualize their own preferences and in turn makes it easy to read and analyse data using VizFrame.

Reference: Documentation - Demo Kit - SAPUI5 SDK

entry.title: Moving Past the AI "Sprinkle": Context, Company Memory & the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
We’ve all seen the wave of enterprise AI tools that feel like a flashy feature layered on top of legacy architecture—what JPMorgan Chase aptly calls "sprinkling AI" onto broken processes. But as SAP laid out during its recent Sapphire event, the future isn't about adding chat boxes to old screens; it’s about a fundamental shift toward an Autonomous Enterprise driven by deep context, untethered orchestration, and multi-agent workflows.

Looking closely at the evolution of Joule Studio 2.0 and SAP’s broader ecosystem partnerships, it is clear that the real value of enterprise AI lies where workflow modernization and organizational data intersect.

The Fallacy of Raw Speed vs. Contextual Accuracy

A major takeaway from recent benchmarks is that while raw code generation in Joule might occasionally take a beat longer than generic AI platforms due to deeper contextual analysis, the actual end-to-end business outcome is delivered up to 10x faster.

This makes complete sense when you look at how Anthropic's Claude models are being woven into SAP Business AI. In finance, procurement, or supply chain workflows, an AI assistant is useless if it hallucinates or lacks an understanding of your underlying data structures. By prioritizing transparency, safety, and deep context over raw, unchecked speed, the system ensures that AI-driven decisions are audit-ready and grounded in enterprise reality.

Going Deeper: Where the Ledger Meets Autonomous Execution

JPMorgan Chase’s modernization journey with SAP perfectly illustrates this philosophy. Instead of treating AI as a separate analytical layer, they are embedding SAP AI agents directly into end-to-end financial workflows.

The goal here is proactive variance detection. Imagine AI agents that catch anomalies before they ever hit the ledger, mapping historical patterns over time to pinpoint the root cause of an issue. For anyone tasked with connecting operational systems to financial databases, this is a massive paradigm shift. It moves AI from a passive tool you ask for reports into an active guardrail that keeps data pristine. Because every action is traceable within SAP’s embedded control framework, it balances speed and scale with essential enterprise trust.

Bridging the Knowledge Gap with "Company Memory"

But how do these AI agents actually know how your specific business operates? This is where the introduction of the Autonomous Suite and Company Memory (built on SAP Signavio) becomes a game-changer.

Company Memory captures the unstructured, institutional knowledge buried in policies, emails, chats, and historical operational data. It extracts what SAP calls "process atoms"—the smallest, reusable units of business knowledge.

My Perspective: This concept of "process atoms" is exactly what has been missing in standard low-code/no-code workflow automations. Instead of hardcoding endless conditional logic loops for every edge case, these atoms act as semantic guardrails. They give custom AI agents—built or extended via Joule Studio 2.0—the exact operational boundaries they need to make smart, independent decisions.

When an anomaly occurs, the agent doesn't just flag a generic error; it references Company Memory to understand why the variance happened and how it was resolved in the past, shrinking resolution times from weeks to mere minutes.

The Path Forward

SAP’s decision to position Joule Studio 2.0 as an open, vendor-agnostic platform supporting third-party models—and making these development capabilities available free until the end of the year—is a massive open door for ecosystem growth.

This shows that enterprise AI is moving past simple chatbots that just answer questions. By grounding AI agents in actual business data and Signavio’s "Company Memory," we are entering the era of the autonomous enterprise. AI is changing from a passive assistant into an active team member that securely runs and fixes workflows alongside us.

 

Reference

SAP. (2026). SAP Sapphire Virtual 2026 dashboard [Webpage]. https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/virtual/flow/sap/sv26/dashboard/page/dashboard

entry.title: RAP Augmentation in Managed Scenario – Enriching Transactional Requests Before BO Processing
entry.content:
Introduction

The RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides several extension points for implementing business logic, such as Determinations, Validations, Actions, and Prechecks. RAP Augmentation adds another extension point that allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework starts processing the business object.

This early execution makes augmentation useful for scenarios where default values, audit information, or business-specific data must be available before any further RAP processing takes place. In this blog, we will understand RAP Augmentation, compare it with Determinations, and implement it using an Employee Management example.

Business Requirement

I had a requirement  an Employee Management application where users create and maintain employee records. During record creation, the application should automatically populate business-specific information instead of relying on users to enter every value manually.

The application should:

  • Set the employee status to PENDING_APPROVAL.
  • Determine whether approval is required based on the selected department.
  • Capture the current user as the creator.
  • Maintain audit information during updates.

Instead of implementing all this logic inside the business object, we will use RAP Augmentation to enrich the incoming request before standard processing begins.

Problem Statement

Determinations are commonly used in RAP to derive or update field values. However, they execute after the request has already entered the transactional buffer.

In some business scenarios, values must be available even before the business object starts processing. For example, default values or audit fields may be required before validations or other business logic execute. In such cases, Augmentation provides a cleaner and more suitable solution by enriching the request at the beginning of the transaction.

What is RAP Augmentation?

RAP Augmentation is an extension mechanism that runs during the Interactive Phase of a RAP transaction. It allows developers to enrich incoming create or update requests before the framework processes the business object.

Rather than modifying persisted data, augmentation focuses on preparing the request by supplying additional values or applying business-specific logic. Once the request is enriched, RAP continues with its normal processing flow.

Augmentation Flow

The request passes through augmentation before reaching the business object implementation. During this stage, additional values can be added or modified, ensuring the business object receives a complete request for further processing.

The overall execution flow is:

  • User sends a create or update request.
  • RAP invokes the registered augmentation.
  • The request is enriched with additional data.
  • Standard RAP processing continues.
  • Data is persisted to the database.

Augmentation vs Determination

Defined at the Projection Behavior Definition using use create/update (augment).Defined at the Interface Behavior Definition using  determination.
Implemented in a Projection Behavior Implementation Class Implemented in the Behavior Pool  class.
Runs during the Interactive Phase, before the BO implementation processes the request.Runs during ON MODIFY or ON SAVE, after data enters the transactional buffer.
Used to enrich or modify the incoming request before BO processing.Used to derive or recalculate values based on data in the transactional buffer.

When Execution timing matter?

The main advantage of Augmentation is when it executes. Since it runs before business object processing, any values added during augmentation are available to the rest of the RAP processing flow.

  • If the application should automatically set an employee's status or determine whether approval is required, these values are already present when the framework performs subsequent processing. If the same logic were implemented in a Determination, the values would only be available later in the transaction.

When to Use Augmentation?

Augmentation is a good choice when the incoming request needs to be enriched before RAP starts processing the business object.

Typical use cases include:

  • Setting default values.
  • Populating audit fields.
  • Deriving values from user input.
  • Performing lightweight request enrichment.
  • Preparing data required by later processing steps.

It is not intended to replace Determinations, which remain the preferred option for business logic that should execute after the request has entered the transactional buffer.

 

BDEF of Interface

managed implementation in class zbp_aky_i_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_i_emp //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zaky_t_emp

lock master

authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create( authorization : global ) ;
  update;
  delete;
  action Approve result [1] $self;
  mapping for zaky_t_emp
{
  Employeeid        = employeeid;
  Firstname         = firstname;
  Lastname          = lastname;
  Department        = department;
  Status            = status;
  Approvalrequired  = approvalrequired;
  Createdby         = createdby;
  Createdat         = createdat;
  Lastchangedby     = lastchangedby;
  Lastchangedat     = lastchangedat;
  }
}

BDEF of Projection

projection implementation in class zbp_aky_c_emp unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for zaky_c_emp //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create( augment );
  use update( augment );
    use delete;
  field ( readonly ) Status;
  field ( readonly ) Approvalrequired;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdat;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedby;
  field ( readonly ) Lastchangedat;
  use action Approve;
}

BP of Projection

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS augment_create FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE zaky_c_emp.

    METHODS augment_update FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE zaky_c_emp.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zaky_c_emp IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD augment_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    IF entity-Department IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Department is mandatory' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_c_emp.        

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid )
      TO failed-zaky_c_emp.          
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp  
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      CREATE FIELDS ( Status Approvalrequired Createdby Createdat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %cid             = entity-%cid
        Status           = 'PENDING_APPROVAL'
        Approvalrequired = COND #(
                             WHEN entity-Department = 'HR'
                               OR entity-Department = 'FIN'
                             THEN abap_true
                             ELSE abap_false )
        Createdby        = sy-uname
        Createdat        = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD augment_update.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).

    MODIFY AUGMENTING ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp   
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Lastchangedby Lastchangedat )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        Employeeid    = entity-Employeeid
        Lastchangedby = sy-uname
        Lastchangedat = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date(  )
      ) ).

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

BP of interface

METHOD Approve.

  " Read current status of the employee
  READ ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zaky_i_emp
    FIELDS ( Status Employeeid )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(employees)
    FAILED DATA(read_failed).

  " Loop and update status
  LOOP AT employees INTO DATA(employee).

    " Only approve if current status is PENDING_APPROVAL
    IF employee-Status <> 'PENDING_APPROVAL'.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = employee-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
                 severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                 text     = 'Only records with PENDING_APPROVAL status can be approved' ) )
      TO reported-zaky_i_emp.
      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = employee-%tky )
      TO failed-zaky_i_emp.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.

    " Update status to APPROVED
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zaky_i_emp IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zaky_i_emp
      UPDATE FIELDS ( Status )
      WITH VALUE #( (
        %tky   = employee-%tky
        Status = 'APPROVED'
      ) )
      FAILED   DATA(update_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(update_reported).

    " Pass back result
    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky   = employee-%tky
      %param = employee
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Output

Click on the create 

After adding the value click on the create and put the debugger on the create-augment method to check it is triggering or not because we are doing managed application where creation is handled by framework

Execute

Currently it is in pending state click on the Approve employee.

After clicking on action

If you not adding the department

 

Conclusion

RAP Augmentation provides a simple and effective way to enrich transactional requests before the RAP framework begins processing the business object. It is particularly useful for populating default values, maintaining audit information, and preparing request data that should be available throughout the transaction.

In this example, we used augmentation to automatically populate employee details while keeping the business object implementation clean and maintainable. Understanding when to use Augmentation instead of Determinations helps developers choose the right extension point and build more maintainable RAP applications

entry.title: Trigger SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from Fiori Application
entry.content:
In this blog, we will learn how to trigger an SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) workflow directly from a SAPUI5/Fiori application.

This approach is useful when users need to start approval processes, requests, or business workflows directly from the application UI.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure the following things are done:

  1. Cloud Identity Services must be subscribed.
  2. SAP Build Process Automation must be subscribed.
  3. SAP Build Work Zone must be subscribed.
  4. Workflow must be deployed successfully.
  5. User must have the role: “ProcessAutomationParticipant”

Note: Workflow triggering from the UI will work after deployment because BPA runtime is accessed through destinations configured in SAP BTP.

You can find the complete implementation here: https://github.com/MaheshSirsat/trigger-bpa-workflow-from-fiori/tree/main 

Step 1 – Fetch SAP Build Process Automation Credentials

We need runtime credentials from the SAP Build Process Automation instance. These credentials will be used while creating the destination.

Navigation

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit.
  2. Go to Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Locate your SAP Build Process Automation instance.

 

  1. Open the instance.
  2. Click View Credentials.

 

 

The credentials contain:

  • API endpoint URL
  • OAuth client ID
  • Client secret
  • Token URL

These details allow our Fiori application to communicate securely with the BPA runtime.

Step 2 – Create Destination

We create a destination that acts as a secure bridge between the Fiori application and the SAP Build Process Automation APIs.

Navigation

  1. Open Destinations
  2. Click Create Destination

 

 

 

Configuration

Name : sap_build_process_automation

URL : endpoints.api

Authentication : OAuth2JWTBearer

Client ID : uaa.clientid

Client Secret : uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL : uaa.url/oauth/token

Additional Properties

 

endpoints : <copy complete endpoints object>

html5-apps-repo : <copy complete html5-apps-repo object>

saasregistryenabled : true

sap.cloud.service : com.sap.spa.processautomation

sap.cloud.service.alias : spa

The destination stores all connectivity information required to call BPA APIs. Instead of hardcoding credentials inside the application, SAP BTP securely manages them through the destination.

Step 3 – Create Fiori Application

Create a SAPUI5/Fiori application that will contain a button to start the workflow.

You can create:

  • Freestyle Application
  • List Report Application

After creation, run the application locally and ensure it opens successfully.

 

Step 4 – Configure xs-app.json

Add the following route inside xs-app.json:

{

  "source": "^/workflow/(.*)$",

  "target": "/public/workflow/rest/$1",

  "destination": "sap_build_process_automation",

  "authenticationType": "xsuaa",

  "csrfProtection": false

}

 

Whenever the application calls:

/workflow/*

the App Router automatically forwards the request to the destination configured earlier.

Step 5 – Create Workflow Trigger Button

Add a button in the UI:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="project2.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page title="Workflow Demo">
        <Button  text="Start WF"  press="onstartWF"/>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

The button allows users to start the workflow directly from the UI.

Step 6 – Implement Workflow Trigger Logic

Create the following function inside the controller:

   onstartWF: async function () {
            var data = {
                definitionId: "myworkflow", // workflow-defination-id
                context: { "name": "mahesh" } // your context
            };
            await $.ajax({
                url: this._getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL() + "/workflow-instances",
                method: "POST",
                async: false,
                contentType: "application/json",

                data: JSON.stringify(data),
                success: function (result, xhr, data) {
                    sap.m.MessageToast.show('Workflow started successfully...')
                },
                error: function (request, status, error) {
                    console.error('Error in Trigger workflow')
                }
            });
        },

 

This code sends a POST request to the BPA runtime API and creates a new workflow instance.

Understanding the Payload

{
    definitionId: "myworkflow",
    context: {        "name": "mahesh"    }
}

definitionId

definitionId: "myworkflow"

This is the workflow definition ID of the deployed workflow.

 

BPA uses this value to identify which workflow should be started.

context

context: {

    "name": "mahesh"

}

This data becomes the workflow context and can be accessed inside forms, approvals, conditions, and automation steps.

Step 7 – Generate Runtime URL

Create the helper method:

_getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
    var appId =this.getOwnerComponent().getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
    var appPath =appId.replaceAll(".", "/");
    var appModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(appPath);
    return appModulePath + "/workflow/v1";
}

 

Instead of hardcoding BPA URLs, the application uses the App Router route configured in xs-app.json.

 

Step 8 – Build and Deploy Application

Build the application:

Deploy it to SAP BTP.

 

The workflow runtime destination and App Router configuration are available only after deployment.

Step 9 – Create Catalog, Group, and Role in Work Zone

After deployment:

  1. Create Catalog
  2. Create Group
  3. Create Role
  4. Assign application tile
  5. Assign role to user

Step 10 – Test from Launchpad

  1. Open SAP Build Work Zone.
  2. Launch the application.
  3. Click Start WF.

Expected result:

Workflow started successfully...

 

You can verify the workflow instance in SAP Build Process Automation Monitoring.

Final Result

We have successfully triggered an SAP Build Process Automation workflow directly from a Fiori application.

✔ BPA credentials are retrieved from the BPA instance.

✔ Destination securely manages authentication and connectivity.

✔ xs-app.json routes requests through App Router.

✔ UI button initiates workflow execution.

✔ Workflow context can be passed dynamically from the application.

✔ Users can start workflows directly from SAP Build Work Zone.
